system_prompt:
You are an expert in OpenFOAM simulation and numerical modeling. Your task is to modify and rewrite OpenFOAM files to fix the reported error. Please do not propose solutions that require modifying any parameters declared in the user requirement, try other approaches instead. You will receive a rewrite_plan. Follow it strictly: only modify files listed in rewrite_plan.target_files and apply only the requested changes. Do not modify files outside the plan. Return the complete, corrected file contents in JSON format: list of foamfile: [{file_name: 'file_name', folder_name: 'folder_name', content: 'content'}]. Ensure your response includes only modified file content with no extra text, as it will be parsed using Pydantic.
Return ONLY valid JSON (no markdown/backticks). Schema: {"list_foamfile": [{"file_name": string, "folder_name": string, "content": string}]}.
user_prompt:
<foamfiles>list_foamfile=[FoamfilePydantic(file_name='controlDict', folder_name='system', content='FoamFile
{
    version     2.0;
    format      ascii;
    class       dictionary;
    location    "system";
    object      controlDict;
}

application     rhoCentralFoam;

startFrom       startTime;

startTime       0;

stopAt          endTime;

endTime         0.2;

deltaT          0.0001;

writeControl    adjustableRunTime;

writeInterval   0.02;

purgeWrite      0;

writeFormat     ascii;

writePrecision  6;

writeCompression off;

timeFormat      general;

timePrecision   6;

runTimeModifiable yes;

adjustTimeStep  no;'), FoamfilePydantic(file_name='fvSchemes', folder_name='system', content='FoamFile
{
    version     2.0;
    format      ascii;
    class       dictionary;
    object      fvSchemes;
}
// * * * * * * * * * * * * * * * * * * * * * * * * * * * * * * * * * * * * * //

fluxScheme      Kurganov;

ddtSchemes
{
    default         Euler;
}

gradSchemes
{
    default         Gauss linear;
}

divSchemes
{
    default         none;
    div(tauMC)      Gauss linear;
}

laplacianSchemes
{
    default         Gauss linear corrected;
}

interpolationSchemes
{
    default         linear;
    reconstruct(rho) vanLeer;
    reconstruct(U)  vanLeerV;
    reconstruct(T)  vanLeer;
}

snGradSchemes
{
    default         corrected;
}

// ************************************************************************* //'), FoamfilePydantic(file_name='fvSolution', folder_name='system', content='FoamFile
{
    version     2.0;
    format      ascii;
    class       dictionary;
    object      fvSolution;
}
// * * * * * * * * * * * * * * * * * * * * * * * * * * * * * * * * * * * * * //

solvers
{
    "(rho|rhoU|rhoE)"
    {
        solver          diagonal;
    }

    U
    {
        solver          smoothSolver;
        smoother        GaussSeidel;
        nSweeps         2;
        tolerance       1e-09;
        relTol          0.01;
    }

    e
    {
        $U;
        tolerance       1e-09;
        relTol          0.01;
    }
}

// ************************************************************************* //'), FoamfilePydantic(file_name='momentumTransport', folder_name='constant', content='FoamFile
{
    version     2.0;
    format      ascii;

    location    "constant";

}

simulationType  laminar;'), FoamfilePydantic(file_name='physicalProperties', folder_name='constant', content='FoamFile
{
    version     2.0;
    format      ascii;
    class       dictionary;
    location    "constant";

}

thermoType
{
    type            hePsiThermo;
    mixture         pureMixture;
    transport       const;
    thermo          hConst;
    equationOfState perfectGas;

mixture
{
    specie
    {
        molWeight   11640.3;
    }
    thermodynamics
    {
        Cp          2.5;
        Hf          0;
    }
    transport
    {
        mu          0;
        Pr          1.0;
    }
}'), FoamfilePydantic(file_name='U', folder_name='0', content='FoamFile
{

// * * * * * * * * * * * * * * * * * * * * * * * * * * * * * * * * * * * * * //

dimensions      [0 1 -1 0 0 0 0];

internalField   uniform (5 0 0);

boundaryField
{
    inlet
    {
        type            fixedValue;
        value           uniform (5 0 0);
    }

boundaryField

boundaryField

// ************************************************************************* //'), FoamfilePydantic(file_name='blockMeshDict', folder_name='system', content='FoamFile

// * * * * * * * * * * * * * * * * * * * * * * * * * * * * * * * * * * * * * //

scale   1;

vertices
(
    // Block 0 - below wedge
    (-0.15242 -0.005 -0.005),   // 0
    ( 0          -0.005 -0.005),   // 1
    ( 0           0        -0.005),   // 2
    (-0.15242  0        -0.005),   // 3

    // Block 1 - above wedge (right side)
    ( 0.000001 -0.005 -0.005),   // 4
    ( 0.3048+0.000001 -0.005 -0.005),   // 5
    ( 0.3048+0.000001  0.1524 -0.005),   // 6
    ( 0.000001       0.1524 -0.005),   // 7

    // Block 2 - above wedge (left side)
    (-0.15242-0.000001 -0.005 -0.005),   // 8
    (-0.15242-0.000001  0.1524 -0.005),   // 9
    ( 0.000001          0.1524 -0.005),   // 10
    ( 0.000001          0        -0.005)   // 11
);

blocks
(
    // Block 0 - below wedge
    hex (0 3 2 1 0 3 2 1) (30 10 1) simpleGrading (1 1 1)

    // Block 1 - above wedge (right side)
    hex (4 5 6 7 4 5 6 7) (60 30 1) simpleGrading (1 1 1)

    // Block 2 - above wedge (left side)
    hex (8 9 10 11 8 9 10 11) (30 30 1) simpleGrading (1 1 1)
);

edges
(
);

boundary
(
    inlet
    {
        type patch;
        faces
        (
            (0 3 2 1)
        );
    }

    outlet
    {
        type patch;
        faces
        (
            (5 6 7 4)
        );
    }

    top
    {
        type patch;
        faces
        (
            (7 6 10 11)
            (10 9 8 11)
        );
    }

    bottom
    {
        type symmetryPlane;
        faces
        (
            (0 1 2 3)
        );
    }

    wedge
    {
        type wall;
        faces
        (
            (3 0 1 2)
        );
    }

    frontAndBack
    {
        type empty;
        faces
        (
            (0 3 2 1)
            (4 5 6 7)
            (8 9 10 11)
            (11 10 9 8)
        );
    }
);

mergePatchPairs
(
);

// ************************************************************************* //')]</foamfiles>

    in file global/fileOperations/fileOperation/fileOperation.C at line 869.

<reviewer_analysis># Error Analysis and Resolution

## Error 1: `log.blockMesh` — Ill-defined primitive entry

**Error Message:**
```
"ill defined primitiveEntry starting at keyword 'vertices' on line 13 and ending at line 22"
```

This is the same error as Attempt 3 — the `vertices` sub-dictionary is malformed. The issue is not about missing commas (those were added in Attempt 3), but something else entirely.

Looking at the current `blockMeshDict` again, I notice the **format of the vertex entries**. In newer versions of OpenFOAM (especially Foundation version), the vertex entries in the `vertices` dictionary must be formatted with **commas separating each complete vertex tuple**, not just spaces. The current format has each vertex on a new line with comments, but the parser expects either all tuples on one line separated by commas, or strict adherence to the older format.

More critically, I suspect the issue is that the vertex entries contain **spaces within the parentheses** that confuse the parser. Looking closely at the current file, the entries look like:

```
(-0.15242 -0.005 -0.005)   // 0
```

The extra spaces before the comment `// 0` might be causing parsing issues. Additionally, the very small offsets (like `0.000001`) combined with the precision of the numbers might be triggering floating-point comparison issues during the face orientation check.

## Proposed Different Approach

Since previous attempts focused on fixing vertex formatting/comma placement and the error persists, let me take a fundamentally different approach:

### 1. Simplify the Geometry — Merge Blocks into One Structured Region

Instead of having three separate blocks that might have subtle topological conflicts, create a **single block** that represents the entire computational domain. Given the user requirement describes a 2D wedge in 3D space, the domain can be represented as a single large hex block that spans from x = -0.15242 to x = 0.3048, y = 0 to y = 0.1524, and z = -0.005 to z = 0.005.

The key insight: The wedge divides the domain into two regions (above and below the wedge plane), plus the wedge itself forms a triangular region. Rather than modeling three separate blocks, model the full rectangular domain as one block, and use **multiple boundary patches** to represent the wedge surface internally.

However, since the user wants a 3D geometry with the specified extents, here's a cleaner approach:

Create a **single block** that covers the entire rectangular prism, and define the wedge as an internal boundary (wall) within that block. This avoids the complexities of multiple adjacent blocks with potential face orientation conflicts.

### 2. Redefine Vertices for a Single Hex Block

Replace the three-block structure with a single hex block covering the full domain:

```
// Vertices for a single block covering the full domain
(-0.15242 -0.005 -0.005),   // 0
( 0.3048   -0.005 -0.005),   // 1
( 0.3048    0.1524 -0.005),   // 2
(-0.15242  0.1524 -0.005),   // 3
(-0.15242 -0.005  0.005),   // 4
( 0.3048   -0.005  0.005),   // 5
( 0.3048    0.1524  0.005),   // 6
(-0.15242  0.1524  0.005)   // 7
);

blocks
(
    hex (0 1 2 3 4 5 6 7) (30 30 2) simpleGrading (1 1 1)
);
```

This creates a single block with dimensions (30×30×2) cells. The resolution is doubled compared to the original attempt (was 30×10×1 and 60×30×1), which provides better accuracy for capturing the shock/shock interaction near the wedge.

### 3. Define Internal Wedge as a Wall Boundary Patch

The wedge surface (where y=0 for x < 0) becomes an **internal wall boundary** within the single block. Update the `boundary` section:

```
boundary
(
    inlet
    {
        type patch;
        faces
        (
            (0 4 7 3)
        );
    }

    outlet
    {
        type patch;
        faces
        (
            (1 2 6 5)
        );
    }

    top
    {
        type patch;
        faces
        (
            (2 3 6 7)
        );
    }

    bottom
    {
        type symmetryPlane;
        faces
        (
            (0 1 5 4)
        );
    }

    wedge
    {
        type wall;
        faces
        (
            // Wedge face: triangle from (0,0,-0.005) to (0,0,0.005) to (-0.15242,0,-0.005)
            (0 3 7 4)
        );
    }

    frontAndBack
    {
        type empty;
        faces
        (
            (0 3 2 1),
            (4 5 6 7)
        );
    }
);
```

The wedge face `(0 3 7 4)` connects vertex 0 (-0.15242,-0.005,-0.005) → vertex 3 (-0.15242,0.1524,-0.005) → vertex 7 (-0.15242,0.1524,0.005) → vertex 4 (-0.15242,-0.005,0.005), forming the vertical wedge surface at x = -0.15242.

### Why This Should Work

- Eliminates the complex multi-block topology issues that caused inward-facing faces.
- Uses a single, well-defined hex block with straightforward vertex ordering.
- Maintains all user-specified domain dimensions and boundary conditions.
- Preserves the 2D nature of the simulation through the `frontAndBack empty` patch.

## Error 2: Cascading Effect

Once `blockMesh` succeeds with this simplified single-block approach, the `rhoCentralFoam` error ("Cannot find file 'points'") will resolve automatically since the mesh will exist.

## Summary of Changes

1. **`blockMeshDict`**: Replace the three-hex-block structure with a single hex block covering the full rectangular domain. Adjust vertex count, block definition, and boundary face connections accordingly.
2. **All other files** (`controlDict`, `fvSchemes`, `fvSolution`, `momentumTransport`, `physicalProperties`, initial/boundary fields in `0/`): No changes needed — they already conform to the user requirements.</reviewer_analysis>
<rewrite_plan>{'target_files': [{'file': 'system/blockMeshDict', 'changes': 'Replace the three-block structure with a single hex block covering the full domain using vertices 0-7 representing the rectangular prism from x=-0.15242 to 0.3048, y=0 to 0.1524, z=-0.005 to 0.005; Set block cell counts to (30 30 2) instead of the three separate blocks; Change inlet boundary face from (0 3 2 1) to (0 4 7 3); Change outlet boundary face from (5 6 7 4) to (1 2 6 5); Change top boundary faces from (7 6 10 11) and (10 9 8 11) to (2 3 6 7); Change bottom boundary face from (0 1 2 3) to (0 1 5 4); Change wedge boundary face from (3 0 1 2) to (0 3 7 4) connecting the four vertices of the wedge surface; Keep frontAndBack as empty with faces (0 3 2 1) and (4 5 6 7)'}]}</rewrite_plan>

<user_requirement>Perform a 2D supersonic flow simulation over a wedge. Create a 3D geometry spanning from -0.15242 m to 0.3048 m in the x-direction, 0 m to 0.1524 m in the y-direction, and -0.005 m to 0.005 m in the z-direction. The simulation starts at 0.0 seconds and runs until 0.2 seconds with a time step (deltaT) of 0.0001 seconds. The results are written every 0.02 seconds. The inlet velocity is 5 m/s, inlet temperature is 1 K and inlet pressure is 1 Pa. The wedge boundary uses slip boundary condition for velocity. Use normalized thermodynamic properties where molWeight is set to 11640.3, Cp is 2.5, Pr is 1.0, and dynamic viscosity mu is 0. This corresponds to a gas constant R of 0.7143 and a specific heat ratio of 1.4. Use rhoCentralFoam solver.</user_requirement>

Please update OpenFOAM files according to rewrite_plan only. Only include files from rewrite_plan.target_files in your output.
